question.description: The issue lacks sufficient detail to understand requirements, reproduce a bug, or determine scope.
question.criteria: Apply when the issue is ambiguous, missing steps to reproduce, or leaves key questions unanswered that block understanding or implementation.
question.action: Post a comment listing the clarifying questions, then apply this label.

bug.description: The issue reports incorrect or unexpected behavior in an existing feature.
bug.criteria: Apply when the issue describes something broken or not working as intended. Look for indicators like 'broken', 'error', 'fails', 'wrong', 'crash', "doesn't work", stack traces, or unexpected output.
bug.action: Apply this label when the issue is a bug report.

enhancement.description: The issue requests new functionality or an improvement to existing behavior.
enhancement.criteria: Apply when the issue asks for a new feature, capability, or improvement to something that already works. Look for indicators like 'add', 'feature request', 'improve', 'new', 'support for', 'would like', 'could we'.
enhancement.action: Apply this label when the issue is a feature or enhancement request.

dependency upgrade.description: The issue requests upgrading a library, package, tool version, or other external dependency.
dependency upgrade.criteria: Apply when the issue is specifically about bumping or updating a dependency to a newer version, whether for security, compatibility, or access to new upstream features.
dependency upgrade.action: Apply this label when the issue is a dependency upgrade request.

do.description: The issue is simple and well-defined enough to complete in a single, easy-to-review commit PR.
do.criteria: Apply when the change is small, the scope is clear, and the implementation path is straightforward with no architectural decisions needed. Examples: fixing a typo, updating a config value, adding a small utility, correcting documentation, a one-line fix.
do.action: Apply this label when the issue can be implemented cleanly in a single commit without prior planning. Mutually exclusive with 'plan'.

plan.description: The issue is complex enough to require design or planning before implementation.
plan.criteria: Apply when the change spans multiple components, requires architectural decisions, involves coordination between systems, or cannot be done cleanly in a single commit. Examples: new multi-step workflows, refactors, new agent types, cross-cutting infrastructure changes.
plan.action: Apply this label when the issue requires a plan before implementation begins. Mutually exclusive with 'do'.

human-required.description: The issue or PR needs a human in the loop — an agent alone cannot (or should not) resolve it.
human-required.criteria: Apply when the work touches something that should not be decided by an agent alone. Examples: security-sensitive decisions (auth changes, secret rotation, permission model changes), production deployments or rollbacks, billing/access/quota changes, legal/compliance/licensing concerns, changes to branch-protection or agent-identity/permissions, or any point at which the agent has escalated because it is blocked, uncertain, or lacks authority to proceed. When applying this label, also assign the issue or PR to the human(s) whose input is needed.
human-required.action: Apply this label whenever a human needs to be in the loop. Agents should apply it to their own issues/PRs when they hit an escalation point; humans can apply it too. It is not mutually exclusive with any other label.

model:haiku.description: The issue is simple enough to be handled by Claude Haiku — the fastest, lowest-cost model.
model:haiku.criteria: Apply when the change is trivial and mechanical: fixing a typo, tweaking a single config value, updating a comment or a small piece of documentation, or a one-line code change with no design decisions. If in doubt between haiku and sonnet, prefer sonnet.
model:haiku.action: Apply this label when the issue is trivial. Mutually exclusive with the other model:* labels — at most one model:* label may be present on an issue. If any model:* label is already present on the issue, do not add or change it.

model:sonnet.description: The issue is a moderate-complexity change best handled by Claude Sonnet — the balanced default for most work.
model:sonnet.criteria: Apply when the change is non-trivial but scoped: implementing a well-specified feature, a straightforward bug fix, a dependency upgrade with obvious next steps, or any 'do'-labeled issue that is more than mechanical but does not require architectural thought. This is the default choice when no other model:* label clearly fits.
model:sonnet.action: Apply this label for typical implementation work. Mutually exclusive with the other model:* labels — at most one model:* label may be present on an issue. If any model:* label is already present on the issue, do not add or change it.

model:opus.description: The issue is complex enough to warrant Claude Opus — the highest-capability, highest-cost model.
model:opus.criteria: Apply when the change requires deep reasoning, cross-cutting design, or careful trade-off analysis: high-level design for a new feature, non-trivial refactors, new agent types, work that spans multiple components, security-sensitive changes, or anything the 'plan' label would also apply to. Ambiguous or under-specified issues that require the agent to reason about scope also belong here.
model:opus.action: Apply this label for complex or design-heavy work. Mutually exclusive with the other model:* labels — at most one model:* label may be present on an issue. If any model:* label is already present on the issue, do not add or change it.
